title: Enhanced Spin-to-Charge Conversion in Bi2Se3/NiFe via Interface Engineering with a Ti Spacer Layer
authors: Sourav Rajat Subhra Maitra, Poulami Manna, Ravi Prakash Singh, Chandrasekhar Murapaka, Arabinda Haldar
abstract: Topological insulators have attracted significant attention in spintronics due to their topological surface states and spin-momentum-locking-driven spin-to-charge conversion. Among these, Bi2Se3 has been extensively investigated because of its large bulk bandgap and single Dirac cone band structure. However, spin-to-charge conversion strongly depends on the quality of the topological insulator/ferromagnet interface. Here, we investigate spin-to-charge conversion in a sputter-deposited heterostructure comprising a topological insulator (Bi2Se3) and a ferromagnetic NiFe thin film separated by a titanium spacer layer. The Bi2Se3 layer is deposited on a silicon substrate for industrial compatibility. Pure spin current is injected into the Bi2Se3 layer through the titanium spacer via spin pumping induced by spin precession during microwave-driven ferromagnetic resonance of the ferromagnetic film. Spin pumping studies are performed by varying the Bi2Se3 thickness. The Gilbert damping parameter exhibits a significant 55% increase at a Bi2Se3 thickness of 4 nm, indicating a pure surface-state contribution. The spin Hall angle, which quantifies the spin-to-charge conversion efficiency, increases by an order of magnitude upon insertion of the titanium spacer layer. This enhancement is attributed to the suppression of interdiffusion between the Bi2Se3 and NiFe layers by titanium, thereby preserving the topological surface states. These findings highlight the important role of titanium spacer layers in spintronic devices based on topological materials.
summary: This work investigates how to boost spin-to-charge conversion in topological insulator spintronic devices by engineering the interface. By introducing a titanium spacer layer between $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3$ and $	ext{NiFe}$, the researchers successfully prevented material interdiffusion, leading to a dramatic, order-of-magnitude enhancement in the spin Hall angle. This highlights the critical role of interface chemistry in realizing high-efficiency topological spintronic devices.
main_problem: The primary goal is to enhance the spin-to-charge conversion efficiency in spintronic devices utilizing topological insulators.
main_result: The insertion of a titanium spacer layer significantly enhances the spin Hall angle (SHA) by an order of magnitude compared to direct interfaces.
method: The study uses spin pumping measurements via Ferromagnetic Resonance (FMR) spectroscopy and measures the resulting transverse voltage using the Inverse Spin Hall Effect (ISHE).
model_or_system: The system is a sputter-deposited heterostructure: a topological insulator ($	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3$) coupled with a ferromagnetic film ($	ext{NiFe}$) separated by a titanium ($	ext{Ti}$) spacer layer, deposited on a silicon substrate.
key_observables: Spin Hall Angle (SHA), Gilbert damping parameter ($\alpha_{	ext{eff}}$), ISHE voltage ($V_{	ext{ISHE}}$), and magnetoresistance ($\Delta\sigma(H)$).
important_parameters: The optimal $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3$ thickness appears to be around 4 nm, and the enhancement factor is an order of magnitude.
main_assumptions: The enhancement is attributed to the $	ext{Ti}$ spacer layer suppressing detrimental interdiffusion between the $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3$ and $	ext{NiFe}$ layers, thereby preserving the topological surface states.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematics of the spin-to-charge conversion setup, FMR spectra showing damping parameter changes with thickness, and ISHE voltage measurements comparing samples with and without the Ti spacer.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares spin-to-charge conversion in $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3/	ext{NiFe}$ versus $	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Se}_3/	ext{Ti}/	ext{NiFe}$ structures, analyzing thickness dependence, quantifying the SHA, and attributing the improvement to interface protection.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter spintronics, the study's reliance on interface engineering, quantum confinement effects (surface states), and spin transport mechanisms (spin pumping) provides relevant experimental benchmarks for understanding open quantum systems dynamics at material interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
